[IMAGE:0]It's just past midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will more than likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

[IMAGE:1]The diagram?  That's the basic footwork diagram from The Academy of the Sword by Gerard Thibault, a 17th century manual of rapier fencing based on sacred geometry (translation available here). That's Thibault over on the right -- a brilliant swordsman and also a close student of Renaissance occultism. He quotes a long passage from Cornelius Agrippa's Three Books of Occult Philosophy in the introductory essay to his book.

[IMAGE:2]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:3]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain other gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I now have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

replies.title: Re: star ruby
replies.content: I haven't practiced the Star Ruby or, for that matter, any of Crowley's rituals, so I don't have any personal experience as to their effects, balanced or otherwise. It does sound as though, for whatever reason, the Star Ruby isn't a good fit for you. You don't have to give up the LBRP just because you find Thelema appealing, however -- as far as I know, Liber O vel Manus et Sagittae remains an officially approved Thelemic system of practice, and it uses the LBRP in its classic GD form -- you can find an online copy here.

replies.title: Re: star ruby
replies.content: Many thanks for this!

replies.title: Re: star ruby
replies.content: You know, I read somewhere that some Thelemites think the Star Ruby was a joke Crowley was playing on some of his students. JMG’s right, Liber O is still very much a part of the curriculum.

replies.title: Re: star ruby
replies.content:
This is a problem in general with Crowley's work: he was a terrific trickster, and loved to pull the wool over people's eyes just to mess with them, and sometimes it's hard to know when he was playing his audience for fools.

I tend to keep his work and his philosophy and his followers at arm's length, but I have a collection of his writings, of which I love to read Magick In Theory and Practice from time to time. One critic said that this book was the most valuable advice and the most worthless dreck all shuffled together and sitting side by side, which I can't really disagree with. :D

---Taupe Orthogonal Raccoon

replies.title: (no subject)

1) In magic, the deities you work with form your basic alphabet of energies. If you use a complete pantheon, you've got a complete alphabet. comments.title: Don´t enter the Light?
comments.content:
A relatively popular "theory" or speculation on various "metaphysical" forums lately is the idea that near-death experiences can be malign, and that one should *not* enter "the Light" (especially not after your physical body really died), since the Light is fake. In the more paranoid versions, the Light is identified with something demonic. I´ve even seen people reference the "Star Trek: Voyager" episode "Coda" in this context! (It´s about an evil space alien who masquerades as a deceased loved one during a near-death experience, in order to lure the soul into its cosmic spider web in order to feast on it, or something to that effect.)

A less paranoid version (Damien Echols) claims that the Light comes from the Ouroboros or the Abyss, and that it hinders the soul from ascending to the divine spheres. Echols claims to have personally experience this during mediations: he saw the Light, entered it, and was immidiately cast down to Earth again.

What´s your take on this? Is there *something* to these speculations, or is it just paranoia, or some kind of strange fad? Note that Echols claims to work with the Golden Dawn material.

Tidlösa, Sweden

Sources (videoclip warning - I know you don´t like videos, so this is for reference only):
First, the Star Trek clip (mostly for fun). Next, the Echols clip, which is rather long. The Ouroboros material I´m referring to is around 32:00 - 37:00, but for context interested people should watch at least 19:00 - 37:00.

replies.title: Re: Don´t enter the Light?
replies.content:
Besides pop entertainment, a lot of this idea about The Light as evil comes from criticism of the New Age movement by Evangelicals who were just as nutty as the people they were "saving" us from. :-O This sort of thing was largely based on their overly-literal interpretation of "Lucifer" as "light bearer". To hear them preach it, anything in the New Age literature pertaining to "light" or "enlightenment" was ipso facto talking about Satan as an angel of light trying to fool clueless seekers. Never mind passages from the Bible about "the Light shineth in darkness" or "those which sat in darkness have seen a great light" or "a light to enlighten the Gentiles" or "yet a little while is the light with you", and I could go on! :) Lucifer is mentioned exactly once in the Bible, in the Book of Isaiah, and it's not at all clear who the prophet is talking about, apart from some important figure who had a great fall. (Babylonian kings are popular subjects for interpretation.) The words in Hebrew, Greek, and Latin in this passage refer to the planet Venus as the morning star; but then again the hymn "How Brightly Shines the Morning Star" was not written about Satan! >:)

Their most ridiculous idea was their insistence that the Buddha is really Satan because "Buddha" means "the enlightened one", therefore Lucifer the Light-bearer, therefore...[Church Lady voice] SAY-TAN? Of course, Buddha and his teachings have to do with "awakening", not "light" in the sense of bedazzlement, which is what they seem to think. A handful of word from Buddhists texts were translated as "enlightenment" in the sense of "clarity of perception", which might have been clear to educated readers a hundred years ago, but tends to be misleading today.

--Taupe Orthogonal Raccoon

Here's some background in regards to my questions. I'm a new college student and live in a college dorm. Ever since I moved in, every so often I've been seeing things appearing in my room. replies.title: Re: Bathing
replies.content: You can stand in a washing up bowl that's big enough for both your feet. I have a bath and still use one as it uses less water and it can all be tipped into the watering can.

replies.title: Re: Bathing
replies.content:
Good heavens, yes. Most African-American homes when this tradition was created didn't have bathtubs -- they were too poor to afford what, in the 19th century, was a luxury item.

Get a large plastic basin -- large enough to stand in, as Anon noted above, is good, but if you can't find one don't worry about it. Make the hoodoo bath in the basin. Soak a washcloth in the mix, wring it out gently, and wash starting at the top of your head and ending with the soles of your feet, dipping and wringing out the washcloth regularly. When you're finished, dry off and get dressed, and then take the whole basin out and dump it on the roots of a good strong tree.

replies.title: Re: Sorry about all the questions
replies.content:
1) If you're going to work with Christopher's manual, use his version. That's a good general rule, btw -- use the specific versions of rituals in whatever text you study. Knowing more than one version of a ritual is not a drawback!

2) I haven't read it -- I finished my work in the Hermetic GD tradition long before it was published. I know people who like it a great deal, however.

comments.title: Skill transfer across incarnations
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

some questions about how skills transfer (or not) across incarnations

1) Is there a way to acquire/practice skills in the present incarnation with the intent of accessing them in the next incarnation? (say I want to have some musical skills in the next (and future) incarnations. Beyond just practicing music in some form in this incarnation, is there something I can do to ensure they actually manifest in the next?)

2) How can I, in this incarnation, access skills I've practiced in previous incarnations? Is there a ritual, or some other practice that would help with this? (of course I assume, I have picked up *some* skills, somewhen!)

3) If someone has a limited time to live, say with a diagnosis of a particularly untreatable cancer (happened to a friend, he has 6 months to a year to live as per his doctor) does it make sense to pick up a magical/devotional/occult system and practice intensely in the time remaining (so he can continue in his next incarnation)?

How would such a practice differ from a more normal/slow paced practice? Just the same practices, but perhaps multiple times a day?

As always,
Thanks in advance,
KaliDasa

replies.title: Re: Skill transfer across incarnations
replies.content:
What passes from one incarnation to another isn't skill, but talent. Mozart is the great example -- he must have spent a dozen lives practicing music, but he still needed to take lessons in playing and composition to turn his talent into skill.

Everything you practice in this life lays down patterns that provide you with a talent you can use in lives to come. Similarly, every talent you have in his life is the result of practice in previous lives. There's no special trick, no ritual, just lots of hard work. I practice music in my present life with this in mind -- I have no talent for music at all, but that won't be true the next time around.

As for your friend, well, remember that all of us have a limited time to live. The only difference is how many years. Systematic practice of spirituality is a good idea in any case, and that builds the most useful of all talents -- the talent for connecting with the higher dimensions of the self and the cosmos. In a situation like your friend's, I'd say it would be best to start at the ordinary pace, and ramp up a little at a time depending on what his health will permit.

replies.title: Re: Skill transfer across incarnations
replies.content:
What a great answer, thank you. Very enlightening.

One last question, if I may,

What ensures that you are born into a circumstance where such talents (laid down by practice in future incarnations) can bloom and be used properly?

a hypothetical example- If someone were to do a lot of practice in mathematics for a few incarnations, if he were born as Einstein in Ulm, Germany in 1879, that sets the stage for appropriate education etc that would trigger this talent. But if the spirit were to incarnate into, say, a Bantu tribe in Africa in the same period, it is hard to envision the math talent developing along the lines as it did for Einstein.

Since many souls have taken thousands of births, and probably have practised something or the other intensely in some of them, I'm guessing that some may lie dormant in the present incarnation if they are not appropriate for the lessons to be learned. So in the hypothetical, the mathematical talent may "sleep" for the Bantu tribesman incarnation?

Are there supernatural entities/Deities/paths of spiritual/magical practice etc that can tilt the scales to more appropriate birth circumstances in future incarnations so talents formed by practice in previous incarnations can develop appropriately in future incarnations? or is it all pretty random?



Thanks in advance,
KaliDasa

PS: I've passed on your advice to my friend with the cancer diagnosis, and he appreciates it very much, and thanks you.

replies.title: Re: Skill transfer across incarnations
replies.content: It's not random at all, but it depends on karma. The more difficult karma you've accumulated for yourself, the more likely you are to be born into a setting where your main job is to work it off, and developing your talents has to take a back seat to that. So pay attention to how you treat other people and how you use whatever gifts you have in this lifetime!

replies.title: Re: Skill transfer across incarnations
replies.content: Thanks, this has been very helpful!

That aside, however, Deuteronomy also forbids people from wearing garments of mixed fibers. Do Christians risk excommunication by wearing socks made of a poly/cotton blend? If not, why should magic be forbidden?

5) One of the most amusing things about modern pop culture is the way that ideas thousands of years old constantly get revived as "brand new ideas." Hindu teachers three thousand years ago proposed that the world of the senses is an illusion...

comments.title: What's Up With Patron Deities?
comments.content:
Yo, I was wondering what was up with patron deities?

Like, if I am a polytheist, then why is there a lot of focus in neopagan world, on this idea that you have to pick a patron deity?

In my experience I've had relationships with a number of different deities. Some I feel called to work with more closely for a time, and then they kind of fade into the background, and another will come forward. At other times, a random Goddess will just pop up, like bam, and I'll learn about her, and she'll be in my life for a hot minute, and then creep back into wood work. Likewise, with different groups I've worked with, I did some work with different deities, because it was part of the group work.

I definitely feel affinities to certain deities but don't think of any of them as my patron. Is this idea just from modern paganism or does it go back to antiquity where some hero was a chosen one of some grand power.

On a related note, in my world view, living in a cosmopolitan world, I can't settle on "just one pantheon" either. Many pantheons call to me at different times. I love reading all mythologies and mythographies. But it seems like in this new neopaganism being "eclectic" is a dirty word. Yet, word passed down from the old school occult street is that occultism itself is eclectic and syncrenestic.

What's your take JMG on patron deities and "just one pantheon" as opposed to "eclectic"? Commentariat, jump into the mix if you wish.

Plum Gibbering Paramecium

replies.title: Re: What's Up With Patron Deities?
replies.content:
I think the idea of everyone having Patron Deities is Neo-Pagan along with only one pantheon.

I follow a Roman-Mesopotamian cultus. After looking into if I could have two different pantheons, I discovered that ancient Polytheists did and also combined Gods. The key is in having separate altars for them all. I have several Deities (including Neptune) who seem to be interested in my devotions.

Neptune it turns out is a family Deity (long story). Meanwhile, my son has been claimed by Odin and Hercules.

I think since modern Neo-Pagans are raised as Monotheists, that their thinking is still colored by singularity and having a special relationship with a Deity.

replies.title: Re: What's Up With Patron Deities?
replies.content:
Hmmm. It could be that the neopagan scene has taken a cue from the monotheist monoculture and has the idea of the patron deity.

Thanks for chiming in! I too have separate altar / devotional areas for different deities.

replies.title: Re: What's Up With Patron Deities?
replies.content: Some people seem to work well with a single patron deity; others don't have one (I don't, for example). With regard to focusing on a single pantheon, that's helpful for magical practice but it's irrelevant in a religious context -- classic Paganism was wide open to gods and goddesses from all over the place. That's why there were temples to Isis in Roman Britain and temples to the Celtic horse goddess Epona wherever the Roman cavalry was stationed -- the entire Imperial cavalry arm adopted her as their patron, alongside whatever other deities they worshipped.

replies.title: Re: What's Up With Patron Deities?
replies.content:

This is cool because I've been working my SoP without deities, but praying to various deities from different pantheons, but without working magic with them per se, at least not in a ritual setting.

I'll keep this distinction in mind, and if I need to do ritual magic with deities, will do the workings in a single pantheon.

I can definitely see the matter of patron deities being very much a personal one -as is so much else in this area.

Thank you as always for your time and attention.

Plum Gibbering Paramecium

replies.title: Re: What's Up With Patron Deities?
replies.content:
On Patron Deities: I also found myself curious about the emphasis on "patron deities" in various books, but my own experience has kinda-sorta borne it out as well. I found Woden interesting and compelling long before I prayed to Him, and once I did, I had a moment of laughing realization when I realized just how suited to his worship I am. That being said, I pray to multiple Gods and Goddesses and have somewhat different relationships with all of them. Particularly relevant for your question, I currently seem to be "on loan" to Freyja - through divination and prayer, I got the message that there are things I need to learn from her that I wouldn't get with as strong a focus on Woden.

On Multiple Pantheons: Just to build on what neptunesdolphins said, worshipping Gods, Goddesses, and spirits from other cultures was extremely widespread in the polytheistic ancient world. The Romans were extremely confused (and then pissed off) that the Jews refused to honor the deified emperor, which was seen as a bare-minimum for your acceptance as part of the empire. Everywhere else, folks were just fine with the deal "keep worshipping your Gods however you want, but for certain civic functions, you need to call on ours."

For an excellent take on polytheism generally, might I recommend our host's World Full of Gods - it was instrumental in pulling me out of atheism and into open-enough agnosticism to have an actual religious experience. YMMV, of course, but it's a great book regardless!

comments.title: Emotional loading
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid, I wanted to ask if occultism (or some branch of psychoogy) promotes any method to reduce the amount of emotional loading in an idea or a concept. By corollary, what would be some ways to increase the amount of emotional loading. Under emotional loading I mean the level of emotional melodrama triggered in the mind by certain abstract concepts or symbols (maybe occultists have an even better term for this?).

Finally, to add to that question, what is the general stance on emotional loading in occultism - is it considered good or bad, or is it dependent on context and circumstances (I am guessing the last option is true)? Based on my pop culture understanding of religions, it seems buddhism appears to oppose emotionality, whereas the Abrahamic religions propose cultivation but not abolition of emotional investment. I am however unaware what traditional occultism teaches on this point.

I would be glad if you could provide me some answers, if you have the time. Otherwise I wish you and the commenters a pleasant and comfortable Magic Monday.

replies.title: Re: Emotional loading
replies.content: From a traditional occult perspective -- or a perspective of simple common sense -- why would you want to increase the amount of melodrama in your life? The occult teaching is that you accept your emotions, and work to transmute dysfunctional emotional states into healthy states, but there's no point to going around whipping yourself up into a frenzy of artificial emoting about something.

replies.title: Re: Emotional loading
replies.content:
Dear Archdruid,

Thank you for the response. I would definitely agree that generally, a reduction rather than an increase in emotional melodrama is needed in the lives of most people. But to present one plausible counterexample - wouldn't an increase in emotionality (maybe not to the level of melodrama, but still...) be a possible benefit for people who are, for instance, completely emotionally detached from reality - to the point of being callous and neglecting basic necessities and relationships? Under such a circumstance, increasing the level of emotional loading, at least to a normal level, would seem beneficial.

I am guessing the topic of "transmuting emotions" is too broad to sum up in a short response, but just as a general answer, would this transmutation work involve more outright affirmations/mantras or instead some kind of work using symbols and rituals?

replies.title: Re: Emotional loading
replies.content:
1) Doing it artificially would be a bad idea. In my experience, people with that kind of detachment usually got there via traumatic childhood experiences that led them to bottle up their emotions, and slapping on a layer of artificial emotion over the repression just means there's more in the way of healing. Getting back in touch with the repressed emotions is far more useful.

2) Neither one. The best system I know of is the one taught by the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, and that uses introspection, reflection, and awareness exercises to do the trick.

replies.title: Re: Emotional loading
replies.content:
Dear Archdruid,

1.) That seems reasonable and does indeed sound like the best way to re-acquire a normal level of emotion.
2.) I did notice that your Octagon Society posts contained some elements matching my question, thanks for the tip. Also thank you for responding in general, I wish you a pleasant Monday.

replies.title: Re: Greek SoP
replies.content:
If I might share some thoughts on the runes.

I seem to remember another commenter mentioning that the runes seemed a bit "insulated" from some of the influence many folks have reported. I can neither support nor refute this claim, as I've been learning the Ogham and have not done many runecastings for several months.

If you are interested in the Runes, wood is the most traditional material, but I'm sure other materials are fine. If you want to go very traditional, you would carve them yourself into wood and then color the runes red, historically this would be done with blood (your own or that of a sacrificial animal). Obviously all of that is not required, but I can tell you that carving your own does forge a very strong mental and emotional link with them.

On materials, it is almost impossible to read about the runes and not run into Edred Thorsson/Stephen Flowers (same guy, Flowers is his given name, Thorsson is his religious/pen name). There's a bit of controversy around him because he has in the past been a part of "Left Hand Path" occult orders, and some folks think this has colored his thinking and writing on the runes in unhelpful ways. I started with him, and his interpretations continue to be an important part of my thinking on the runes, but I've found it helpful to compare with other sources.

Kveldulf Gundarsson was recommended to me by another commenter here, and I found the guided meditations/scrying topics to be extremely helpful. If you'd like to start with less symbolic baggage and make your own associations, Rudiments of Runelore or The Rune Primer give very basic meanings. A good list of books on the runes can be found here: https://norse-mythology.org/runes/the-best-books-on-the-runes/

Summary: if you'd like to get started learning the runes as a method of divination, I'd recommend you pick up The Runecaster's Handbook by Edred Thorsson and draw the staves on some popsicle sticks (maybe cut in half) with a red sharpie. If you find they work for you, you can make/buy some nicer ones.

Good luck, and please let me know if there's anything I can help with.

replies.content:
Though I haven't played in a while, I remain a fan of the 90's version of the lore (strted taking itself too seriously and too much cruft added on in novels and the like for my taste!). Though the chaos gods themselves cannot be directly controlled or banished, their servants (demons) can be by agents of the Emperor of Mankind (a massively powerful psychic worshipped as a god and kept alive by technology). The influence of the chaos gods is also kept at bay by religious devotion to the Emperor, and when demons manifest in the material world, they can be shot or blown up (it is a miniature wargame, after all).

Most of it the Emperor-religion is presented as thinly re-worked Christianity of a militant/gothic flavor. As an example, there is an order of psychic, genetically-engineered monastic super soldiers called "The Grey Knights" (official name: the Chamber Militant of the Ordo Malleus, the wing of the Inquisition tasked with fighting demons) whose whole job is to show up and beat up demons when they come through into this world.

The other thing that comes to mind is that the same gods are featured in the fantasy version of the game, and there, demons and the chaos gods can be opposed by other gods and their followers. That tends to take various historically-influenced forms, from the deified-founder worshippers of the Empire to the Arthurian-inspired followers of the Lady of the Lake in Bretonnia. All of which is to say that I think any sort of real-world positive spiritual influences/practices ought to help.

comments.title: Essenes Question
comments.content:
JMG, the order of essenes discusses focusing intensely on the one thing you desire and putting all your imaginative and material energy into attaining that one thing (to cut a long story short).

On the one hand, what I have often found is that when focusing or concentrating too intently on something, it is like squeezing a bar of soap too tightly: it is liable to simply slip from your hand. Quite often I have had more "success" attaining something when in a state of 'disinterest'--it is more likely to come 'naturally'.

On the other hand, perhaps the one thing I have previously focused on intently has been too narrow so to speak. One needs to focus more on the abstract. As a banal example: wanting intently the bar of soap leads to it slipping from your hand when one should want instead "cleanliness" (a higher level of abstraction).

So there could be two levels here:
- the intense desire of the thing in the abstract
- the calm, disinterested pursuit of the particular

Do you know what I mean?

replies.title: Re: Essenes Question
replies.content: Yes, I do indeed know what you mean. Congratulations -- you've learned one of the lessons that the New Thought movement generally never quite managed to grasp.

I came to a similar conclusion - that one can ardently desire something *if* it is in line with right principles and as long as our ego is not involved in the desiring. I find your summary a bit more clearheaded than my baroque reasoning, so thank you for sharing this.

comments.title: Compost burial
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

My wife watches a youtube series called "Ask a mortician", and yesterday she was watching one about a new type of funeral service, which I happened to watch with her as it was interesting.

Basically, the body is placed in a pod containing microbe enriched soil, and covered with wood chippings and favourite plant cuttings. The climate inside the pod is made warm and humid with high oxygen, to promote decay. The body then, remarkably, decays completely to compost in around 30 days, even including the teeth, nails and bones. The compost can then either be taken away by the family or sent to a nearby forest.

This sounds like quite a nice idea, assuming it all works as planned, but I asked a while back about whether natural burial sites could be prone to etheric ghosts due to the lack of the typical preservative steps done in a funeral parlour. You replied in the affirmative, and I felt that cremation may be more the way to go. However, as the body is literally reduced to compost and therefore utterly transformed in this method, would that break down the etheric body enough that one would pass on quickly? The company was called 'Recompose' if you want to read more about it. (This isn't a sales pitch, I promise! The service isn't even currently available in my country!)

It's meant to be fairly low energy to perform and seems preferable to the alkali hydrolysis method developed recently (something about the 'clinical-ness' of that really puts me off), and obviously returns the nutrients to the ground. Are there any disadvantages you can think of from an occult perspective though?

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Compost burial
replies.content: No disadvantages whatsoever. This sounds like a very good idea, as it allows the body to decompose in a natural way. Mond you, you'd get etheric ghosts in the area, but they wouldn't last long.

comments.title: Nuin
comments.content:
I recently took up Druid study (DMH), after a couple of years of working with the Cabala. Have to say it really suits me.

A few days ago I was floundering for a meditation topic and Nuin popped into my head. I had just reviewed its meanings the day before, from divination, and felt like I could wing it, so I dove in. But the next day I went a little deeper, and started worrying about the discrepancy between your system and the Murray deck that I had just purchased. I feel like Nuin popped into my head specifically for this reason, so I spent some time on it. In the end I decided that I'd start a new meditation series on Saille as the 5th card of the 1st aicme. So goodbye, Nuin, for the next week or two! (At the same time I decided I would work my way up from Earth to Spirit in each aicme.)

Saturday was my first day of meditation on Saille, and I drew a 3-ray Ogham spread to help me get a grip on her. Left card/ray of knowledge was Ailim, right card/ray of power was Beith, reversed, and the central card/ray of peace was Ur. The left and central card were easy enough to relate to Saille from where I was, but Beith, reversed, was a challenge at first. In the end I viewed it as basically an endorsement of placing Saille at the other end of the aicme. Sort of a, "this is the opposite of what you're looking at, so roll on, brother," sort of thing.

Do you see anything there I might have misunderstood? I'm actually fairly amazed at how comfortable I am with Ogham already, after nearly 1000 charts of Geomancy, tarot, and the Druid Animal Oracle (combined, but mostly geomancy).

I’ve been experimenting with numerology, drawing upon The Complete Illustrated Book of the Psychic Sciences by Walter and Litzka Gibson as my primary source. In that book, they give correspondences between the numbers and the planets, to wit:

  1. Sun

I’ve been doing some digging myself, and the earliest source I could find using the Gibsons’ planetary associations was Sephariel’s 1911 The Kabala of Numbers—interesting, while the planetary associations match, the other correspondences don't, quite!—but I haven’t managed to determine if the system was original to him or not.

I was wondering if you happened to know where the modern “Pythagorean system” of numerology originates, or if you have any pointers on where I might try continuing my search?

comments.title: On children
comments.content:
What are the occult teachings around conception and pregnancy? How do pre-birth or pre-conception communications fit with these teachings?

How does one determine whether a child is 'right' for them in their current incarnation?

replies.title: Re: On children
replies.content:
1) That would take a good-sized book to explain. The very short form is souls reincarnate in the body, family, etc. that best fits their karma at that time and the overall destiny of the soul. Until a soul reaches a fairly high level of spiritual attainment, this isn't a conscious thing and doesn't involve choice -- for the soul, it's as automatic as sneezing, though there are spiritual entities that supervise the process.

2) I have yet to encounter supposed pre-birth or pre-conception communications that didn't impress me as a complete load of hooey. It's possible that there are counterexamples, but there are a lot of frauds out there, and even more nitwits.

3) Do you mean whether it's right to reproduce? Most people do it by intuition, or simple blind passion; you can use divination if that's something you know how to do. On the other hand, if you mean whether a particular soul is "right," neither the parents nor the child have any conscious say in that -- it's a function of karma and destiny.

replies.title: Re: Bardo realm, lights
replies.content: Don't worry about it. The after-death state is not boobytrapped -- you've been through it countless times, though you don't happen to remember that now, and there are entities waiting to help you whether or not you've worked with a deity. If you practice occultism, furthermore, your chances of having some degree of consciousness in the after-death state are much higher, and that means you'll be able to get through the immediate after-death state promptly and get to work reflecting on your life and settling into the conditions of the astral sub-plane where your life and character have brought you.

comments.title: Cemetery dirt
comments.content: The book that I've used as a guide to getting a Santa Muerte practice going suggested, as an optional item on the altar, a dish of cemetery dirt.  They made an interesting case as to why this would be suitable on an altar to Our Lady of the Holy Death but I've read other sources (not Santa Muerte related) that strongly discouraged having dirt from a cemetery.  I'm not sure what to think, and would like to know what you recommend as far as graveyard dirt goes.

replies.title: Re: Cemetery dirt
replies.content: Graveyard dirt is an essential ingredient for most traditions that have African roots -- it's the connection to the ancestors and to the land of the dead, which is the flip-side of the land of the living. If you're working with a goddess of death irt seems very sensible to have some.  I've collected some and used it for certain purposes while I was doing my hoodoo training. The basic rules, as I was taught, were (a) always pay for it, by pressing silver coins down into the soil, and pouring some good whiskey, and (b) leave the cemetary by a completely different route than the one you took to get there.

replies.title: Re: Cemetery dirt
replies.content: About b) the cemetery I'd use is the Catholic cemetery where much of my family is buried.  It is gated, with only one road leading in and out (that I'm aware of, anyway).  Leaving by a completely different route might be a little difficult, though I could turn left instead of right as I'm exiting the gate and take a different route home.  Would that work?

replies.title: Re: Cemetery dirt
replies.content: That should be fine. It's the route to and from the cemetery that needs to differ -- you can leave and return to your home by the same door, too.
